learning_outcomes.description: Identify and recall the principles and rules of property law.
Identify and apply property law principles in analysing particular factual situation.
Analyse and interpret relevant case law and statutes relating to property.
Demonstrate skills in written communication (including organising and structuring ideas) in the context of providing advice on legal issues related to property law.
Collaborate in a team to analyse property law issues, provide legal advice and reflect on professional practices.

assessments.task_description:
Your team will produce a 1,500 word ±10% (excluding footnotes, headnote and recommendations) memorandum of advice addressed to the partner at Corrs Chambers Westgarth, advising on the legal issues arising from the Thornfield Commons scenario. The memorandum should: 

  • identify and define the key legal issues arising from the scenario; 
  • apply the relevant property law principles, case law and statute; 
  • develop clear, well-reasoned legal advice and specific recommendations; and 
  • be formatted as a professional legal memorandum addressed to a senior partner of Corrs Chambers Westgarth. 

Format: 12-point Arial or Times New Roman font, 1.5 line spacing, with appropriate headings. All citations must comply with AGLC4.
assessments.submission_guidelines: All submissions are made through Cadmus (via Blackboard). For this team assessment piece, one member submits on behalf of the group. However, all team members must review and agree on the final version before any submissions. Each student is responsible for submitting their own individual components to the team's final submission.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Partner Briefing: Revised Legal Analysis and Advice
assessments.task_description:
Your team must record and submit an 8–10 minute video simulating an internal team meeting responding to the new facts. In the discussion, your team should: 

  • analyse how the new facts change or affect your original legal reasoning and recommendations; 
  • provide revised legal advice and recommendations; and 
  • use professional oral communication appropriate for a work context. 

Your discussion should focus on demonstrating your team’s legal reasoning, adaptability, and collaborative decision-making rather than delivering a scripted presentation. All team members must actively participate in the discussion. Each student’s face and voice must be clearly visible and audible in the video.
assessments.submission_guidelines: All submissions are made through Cadmus (via Blackboard). For this team assessment piece, one member submits on behalf of the team. However, all team members must review and agree on the final version before any submissions. Each student is responsible for submitting their own individual components to the team's final submission.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
You will individually reflect (400-500 words) on your learning and professional development working in a team to solve a legal scenario. Your reflection should: 

  • Analyse your personal and team contributions by drawing on:  
  • your experiences completing the task; 
  • how you enacted professional, personal, and/or institutional values; and 
  • evidence from your progress checks (i.e., sentiment checks, self- and/or peer evaluation), 
  • to produce insights into how you have grown as a developing legal professional (e.g. legal reasoning, communication, collaboration). 

Format: Formal written reflection. Use 12-point Arial or Times New Roman font and 1.5 line spacing. First person language is appropriate.
assessments.submission_guidelines: All submissions are made through Cadmus (via Blackboard).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: Any written or printed material is permitted in the exam. Material may also be annotated. The exam will comprise up to four questions, which may be compulsory. One or more of the questions may be broken down into individual parts. The examination paper will indicate the proportion of the marks given to each question (and to individual parts within a question where applicable). The examination paper may consist of problem-style and/or essay-style questions, or mixed problem- / essay-style questions, of varying weightings and length. Any of the questions may combine material from different aspects of the examinable topics in the course. Further information about the content, format and marking criteria of the final examination will be provided on Learn.UQ once the Chief Examiner has approved the examination paper.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
